sentences.text: My name is Pincus Kolender.
P-I-N-C-U-S This is English.
My Hebrew is P-I-N-C-H-U-S.
They pronounce Pinchus.
Kolender is K-O-L-E-N-D-E-R.
Same name.
No.
No nicknames.
February the 8th, '26.
I'm 70 now.
The city is Bochnia, B-O-C-H-N-I-A, Poland.
My father's name was Chiel, C-H-I-E-L.
He was an accountant, his profession.
He was a very pious man, very religious, ultra, almost ultra religious.
He had a beard and he was a very religious man.
My mother's name was, for a moment there, I went Rebecca.
Rachel, I'm sort of confused a little bit.
Rachel was her name.
Rebecca was my grandmother.
No, she was a housewife.
She was very kind, lovely, and sweet.
I had.
I had-- my brother's name was Abraham.
He perished in Auschwitz.
He was with me in Auschwitz for two years.
By the end, during the death march, he disappeared.
He must have been shot.
He couldn't make it.
And my sister's name was Rose.
She went to Treblinka in 1942.
Never saw her again.
My brother was five years older than I.
He would have been 75 now and my sister was three years older.
I was the youngest.
Well, cousins and relatives, yes, we had.
Also in Bochnia, yeah.
Warm, very warm house-- unit, family unit, very close, very close.
Number 15 Kowalska, K-O-W-A-L-S-K-A, yes.
No, it was a house.
We rented it.
Just a single family house.
Mostly people we knew each other.
It was also very close, very close.
Everybody knew each other.
And they had the shul there, the school, everything was in the same neighborhood.
Yes, there was non-Jews there, too, yes.
In Bochnia, in the elementary school.
And then I went also to Yeshiva, which was a Hebrew Academy.
It was afternoon, yeah.
After school I went to Hebrew, that's it.
Yes, we did.
Yes, quite a lot in Poland, especially in the elementary school.
The children, you know, always picked on Jewish kids.
We always had to stick together to fight back.
Well, it was a way of life.
Since a little boy, I remember that.
So it was a way of life.
You accepted it.
Very active, very vibrant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had the organizations, they had the Zionist organizations, and then they had the Hasidic movement and so many different organizations.
Zionists-- had the Hashomer Hatzair, you know, and the Mizrahi gather.
And then they had the [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH] Hasidim.
It was a very vibrant city.
I believe before the war, there was almost about 5,000.
The city was about 25,000.
In all, in general, it was about 25%, 20% to 25% was Jewish population.
Most of them.
Yes.
Only the wealthy ones lived a little spread out.
Yes, I had bar mitzvah in Europe.
It wasn't like here.
It was plain.
Just went to shul, made the blessing, I had the blessing, and my father gave some vodka, the vodka and sweet cake and that was it.
And here is almost like a wedding.
I was very surprised when I came here when I saw this, what kind of bar mitzvahs they have here.
It was plain, small.
Small building with about 100, 120 people.
We had several of them and-- and all over spread out.
Ours was maybe about, what, 100 to 120 people-- small building.
Well, we noticed in 1938 the situation got very tense.
We knew about Hitler and there was already fear about Hitler, he may invade Poland.
So the situation got a little tense but we never thought of something like that will happen, and a lot of people who could afford it started to leave.
But very few people could afford it, go to America, or to Israel, and different countries.
Unfortunately we couldn't.
My parents couldn't afford it.
Well, from reading the paper, on radio.
So, you couldn't help it.
Yes, in 1938 when the first batch.
You know, he deported all the Polish Jews who lived in Germany.
That was in 1938.
As a matter of fact, my uncle, my father's brother and sister lived in Berlin and they were deported.
So they came in 1938.
Well, they still had a lot of good things to say because the Jews lived very comfortable in Germany before the war.
And they were very disappointed, but they had only good things to say about Germany-- about the population.
But things had changed in 1938, like Hitler.
It was-- Hitler just brainwashed his people, and many said-- had friends, good friends, and they turned, just they became enemies.
Unfortunately, that's the way it was in Germany.
Very sad, and-- and we were helpless.
We couldn't do anything.
It was very helpless, a bad situation.
You mean before they came to Poland, before the Nazis?

Well, like I said, the situation was very tense and we had to be very careful not to-- I don't know what, my father, I remember, he was thinking about leaving and he was trying so hard to leave and it just, there was no way.
He couldn't get a visa, and-- and there wasn't enough money to leave.
And that was very-- it affected us, because we knew the situation-- it's going to get worse before it gets better.
Any what?
Laws, well, there were, you know, the Polish government was very anti-Semitic, too.
For instance, my father was a veteran during World War I and anything if you want to get it-- by the end of the war, my father had to give up the accounting.
He worked for some Gentiles and they cancelled-- cancelled the job.
So he opened up a little store just by the end of the war.
You know, in Poland, they had monopoly for cigarettes.
You had to have permission.
And as a veteran, he went to Krakow, which was the capital there, and he couldn't get it.
Everybody else could, Gentiles could get the permission.
He couldn't even though he was a veteran.
And that, I remember, he was always talking about it.
He was very upset about it.
The beginning was fairly quiet.
You see, the Germans, they were masters of deception.
When they came into Poland, they didn't start killing Jews.
Although there was sporadic, you could hear from time to time they arrested someone for something, for offenses.
But they didn't come and kill them in mass murder.
So it was quiet.
So people thought, well, maybe things will settle down, the war will be over, and everything will be fine.
But gradually, that's the way they deceived us.
They make us believe everything would be all right, and then they put us into ghettos by 19-- end of 1940.
And once you were in ghetto, you were trapped.
People couldn't do anything no more, because the ghetto over there wasn't like here.
You know, they put fences around, and guards.
And you couldn't go in, you couldn't go out.
If you went out without permission, you were shot.
There was no-- no questions.
Yes, by the end of 1940 the decree came out.
Every Jew from 12 years old or so, 12 or 14, I don't remember, had to wear a star.
It was compulsory.
If you didn't wear, you were punished and were arrested, and sometimes you were shot, too, if you didn't wear.
No, I don't remember but I do remember it was in 1940.
It was in winter.
It was cold, this I remember.
Yes, fortunately for us, the ghetto we didn't have to move because where we lived it was the ghetto.
So we didn't have to move from our house.
In winter of 1940, I remember that.
Well, first of all, I couldn't go to school.
I was a-- Jewish kids couldn't go to school.
We had to have our own school.
And you were restricted.
We couldn't go to parks or movies, or things like that.
Everything, you had to stay in the ghetto and so your life was restricted a lot.
And even we-- we had to be careful in the synagogues.
They were restricted, you see.
We couldn't-- everything was to be secretly in the synagogue.
Because only four, they had a law of decree, no more than four people can gather.
So every time when we went to shul, to synagogue, we had to stay outside.
Somebody had to guard and watch it, if they-- if they, you know, the Gestapo doesn't come.
And the school.
I had no school.
I had to-- I remember my parents, they had a tutor, tutorer, who taught me Polish, and-- because I was still a young boy, 13 some years old.
And I didn't finish.
So I-- Yiddish and Hebrew, I learned by this woman teacher.
Oh, ration, yes.
Yes, and by '40 they had rations, right.
Everybody, you're allowed only so much.
Well, what happened, I went to work.
I was healthy.
I was strong.
And so we went to work out-- out of the ghetto.
We built highways.
And the Germans already prepared in 1940 for the Russian offensive.
So they built already highways, that then they started.
So, since I was young and strong, I volunteered.
I figured once we went, the trucks came and picked us up and we worked out of the city in little towns.
So we could get from the farmers eggs, milk, and bread on the black market.
And it was cheaper.
In the ghetto, you couldn't get anything.
So we-- I used to bring, everyday, when I had the chance, food for the family.
So we had plenty to eat.
So we had enough.
I wouldn't say plenty but, yes, we had enough to sustain ourselves.
Well, when we worked, we had an hour lunch.
So we went from-- at that time, there wasn't much restriction.
So we went to the farmers and we contacted them.
And I remember we used to buy off them chickens and eggs.
And we paid them for it.
There was money available, just the money couldn't get-- in the ghetto-- anything for the money.
And so that's the way it went for about a year.
Even though I worked very hard, but at least I came home every evening and I brought food for the family, so everybody was happy.
At that time, it was, like I say, by 1942 they were very strict.
You couldn't-- they searched you, if they caught you, you-- you could have been arrested and even executed.
But the first year, they didn't have any restriction on that.
Little towns.
I do remember this Klai, this I remember, that name.
K-L-A-I and some other little towns.
I couldn't remember the names anymore.
My father, at that time, we still had the grocery store but there was nothing to sell because he couldn't get it.
So he didn't do anything.
He was also, he was very religious.
So he was just sitting and studying the Torah.
No, they didn't.
The work for me?
Well, like I said, we were picked.
There was about 50 or 60 young boys and we were assigned to this job.
And everyday they came, German civilians.
They picked us up and we worked with them which they were fair.
The civilians weren't as cruel as the SS.
All we had to do, as long as we did our job, that they didn't bother us.
By 1941, they established a Judenrat, a Jewish council.
Well, it was about 10 or 12 members and I remember the president, his name was Simche Weiss, W-E-I-S-S.
I met his son in Israel.
His son survived and I met him.
Yes, there was some violence because the Gestapo used to come in.
A lot of people lived illegally.
They didn't have no papers and often they went in-- in civilian clothes.
People didn't know.
And then they stopped people, and if you didn't have any papers to prove that you're legal here, and they arrested them and we never heard of them again and never saw them again.
19-- the end of-- I believe it was the end of 1940 to 1943.
It changed a lot, like I say.
Always fear, you had to be careful when you go on the street.
Less you walked in the street, the better.
You had to stay at home most of the time.
And, of course, the news was very bad.
As it happened, our ghetto, the SS commander wasn't too bad.
He was bribed.
You know, so he took bribes.
But we heard from other cities that there was going on a lot of killing and executions.
So there was a lot of fear.
You never knew, it might happen the same thing in our ghetto.
The commander, I remember his name was Joseph Miller.
And he was captured after the war.
I think he got life in prison in Germany.
We were living in the same house, of course, we had to take in-- we had to take in other people because there was so crowded.
So we took in families, whole families.
We had two bedrooms.
We were five-- we had to take in another five people.
I remember his name was Lipsky.
That's all I remember, Lipsky.
They had, yes, two children.
And I don't know what happened to them.
Well, he was one of them.
He was a relative of us, relation.
Yes, they were searching for a lot of merchants who had fabrics, you know, merchandise.
Everything had to be turned in to the Germans and not everybody did.
So we always had people who squealed on them, you know.
They came in and searched.
If they found, they took the whole family and execute them.
Very serene, quiet.
Every store was closed.
I mean, people went to synagogue.
Like you see, ever been in Israel?
You ever been to Bnei Brak?
Same thing.
We probably couldn't get any candles.
I couldn't remember that.
I don't, I don't know.
Well, everybody had to register from the beginning.
You had to have a indication card, so everybody had to register with a number.
That was in '40, 1940.
In '41, started in '41 and '42 and '43.
Well, there was a lot of people, they came out and they-- the-- the police came and they had the names.
And they came to the houses, hey, this party had to, by tomorrow, they have to come to the railroad station, and they have to report.
And that's the way they did from the beginning, But the third-- the third one, the last one, which was the largest one-- that was in 1943-- they came, and they-- with a whole company of SS came.
And they came with loud speakers, I remember.
And they gave orders-- every house, everybody, they gave you so much time.
We had to report to that one place-- it was a special place where everybody had to report.
And they told us right away anybody within, let's say it was 10 o'clock, if he doesn't show up, and if they found them, they would be executed on the spot.
So most of the people did report to the, you know, to the place.
But many of them did not.
And I was-- And then they selected people.
They put them on trucks-- on-- put them to the railroad station.
They left 150 people.
My father was a good friend of the president of the Jewish Judenrat, so he allowed him to stay.
They put up a list of 150 people who can stay in the ghetto.
So we were lucky one and they allowed us to stay.
But I remember next morning, SS came and they start counting.
Unfortunately, there was more than 150.
There was about 200 people, because they-- you know, the Judenrat, you know, they had a lot of friends and just tried to put in more people as possible.
So I remember the SS man, his name was Haase-- H-- can never forget this name, H-A-A-S-E.
Pronounced it Haase.
He was a sturmfuhrer-- he was in charge.
And I could hear him.
He was angry, very angry.
He said, I gave orders for 150-- and here you've got 200.
And he came and he started picking, 50 people, picked them up.
My mother was among them, and they took them about 50 feet further from us and lined them up, and we could see, and they executed them all right in front of us.
That was September the 3rd.
It was on a Friday, 1943.
And then they came and picked 20 guys, young guys.
We had to take the corpses and put them on a place.
And they told us we had to cut up wood, and put them up on the piles.
And they gave us gasoline, poured on them and we burned the bodies.
And that was also the same day.
And I remember that was in the evening.
So I tried to pull out my mother's body.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Just one-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
There was grass, and I tried to pull it out.
And I thought, maybe, they wouldn't see it and I'd be able to bury the body later at night.
And this SS man was there, guard, and he noticed that and he came up to me and kicked me right, kicked me so hard and he said, what is she-- what are you doing?
And they told me to put back the body, and I had to burn the whole, it was a whole 50 people.
We burned them.
But after the SS man left, I picked up a can, a metal can, and I took a bunch of ashes and I put them in the can.
And I remember, I buried it right next-- there was a fence there, and I buried it.
I figured if I survive maybe I'll be able to come back and bury, give her a burial.
I was there eight years ago.
And we went to the place, but unfortunately, everything looks so different.
First-- it's overgrown with weeds, about 10 feet tall.
The fence is gone.
And I-- I spent there a couple hours.
I couldn't locate that place.
But I really had hoped that maybe I'll find that metal, with the ashes.
But I'd have to spend probably days to find it if it's still there.
So that was, and so my father and my brother were the only survivors.
We were in the ghetto another two, two months.
We had to liquidate, liquidate everything.
The furniture-- we had-- the Germans came with trucks, and we had to unload all the furniture, all the belongings from the houses, and they sent it away to Germany.
So I was there another two months from September to middle of November.
And then middle of November they came and picked.
We were 150 people, and just picked 100 people at random.
They put us on trains and sent us to Auschwitz.
And like I said, my brother and myself in Auschwitz all this time.
And my-- yeah, and my father was left in the ghetto.
That's it.
Yeah.
